images.slide_text:
Introduction to WirelessBenefits of Wireless
A Wireless LAN (WLAN) is a type of wireless network that is commonly used in homes, offices, and campus environments.
WLANs make mobility possible within the home and business environments.
Wireless infrastructures adapt to rapidly changing needs and technologies.

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Top Level\_imgs_wlan\slide6_shape2.png
images.slide_text:
Introduction to WirelessWireless Technologies
Bluetooth – IEEE WPAN standard used for device pairing at up to 300ft (100m) distance.
Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) – Supports mesh topology to large scale network devices.
Bluetooth Basic Rate/Enhanced Rate (BR/EDR) – Supports point-to-point topologies and is optimized for audio streaming.
WiMAX (Worldwide Interoperability for Microwave Access) – Alternative broadband wired internet connections. IEEE 802.16 WLAN standard for up 30 miles (50 km).

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Top Level\_imgs_wlan\slide6_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
Introduction to WirelessWireless Technologies
Bluetooth – IEEE WPAN standard used for device pairing at up to 300ft (100m) distance.
Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) – Supports mesh topology to large scale network devices.
Bluetooth Basic Rate/Enhanced Rate (BR/EDR) – Supports point-to-point topologies and is optimized for audio streaming.
WiMAX (Worldwide Interoperability for Microwave Access) – Alternative broadband wired internet connections. IEEE 802.16 WLAN standard for up 30 miles (50 km).

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Top Level\_imgs_wlan\slide7_shape2.png
images.slide_text:
Introduction to WirelessWireless Technologies (Cont.)
Cellular Broadband – Carry both voice and data. Used by phones, automobiles, tablets, and laptops.
Global System of Mobile (GSM) – Internationally recognized
Code Division Multiple Access (CDMA) – Primarily used on the US.
Satellite Broadband – Uses directional satellite dish aligned with satellite in geostationary orbit. Needs clear line of site. Typically used in rural locations where cable and DSL are unavailable.

images.slide_text:
Introduction to WirelessRadio Frequencies
All wireless devices operate in the range of the electromagnetic spectrum. WLAN networks operate in the 2.4 and 5 GHz frequency bands.
2.4 GHz (UHF) – 802.11b/g/n/ax
5 GHz (SHF) – 802.11a/n/ac/ax

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Top Level\_imgs_wlan\slide13_shape2.png
images.slide_text:
WLAN ComponentsWireless NICs
To communicate wirelessly, laptops, tablets, smart phones, and even the latest automobiles include integrated wireless NICs that incorporate a radio transmitter/receiver.
If a device does not have an integrated wireless NIC, then a USB wireless adapter can be used.

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Top Level\_imgs_wlan\slide14_shape2.jpg
images.slide_text:
WLAN ComponentsWireless Home Router
A home user typically interconnects wireless devices using a small, wireless router.
Wireless routers serve as the following:
Access point – To provide wires access
Switch – To interconnect wired devices
Router  - To provide a default gateway to other networks and the Internet

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Top Level\_imgs_wlan\slide15_shape2.png
images.slide_text:
WLAN ComponentsWireless Access Point
Wireless clients use their wireless NIC to discover nearby access points (APs).
Clients then attempt to associate and authenticate with an AP.
After being authenticated, wireless users have access to network resources.




Cisco Meraki Go access points

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Top Level\_imgs_wlan\slide16_shape2.png
images.slide_text:
WLAN ComponentsAP Categories
APs can be categorized as either autonomous APs or controller-based APs. 
Autonomous APs – Standalone devices configured through a command line interface or GUI. Each autonomous AP acts independently of the others and is configured and managed manually by an administrator.
Controller-based APs – Also known as lightweight APs (LAPs). Use Lightweight Access Point Protocol (LWAPP) to communicate with a LWAN controller (WLC). Each LAP is automatically configured and man

images.slide_text:
WLAN ComponentsWireless Antennas
Types of external antennas:
Omnidirectional – Provide 360-degree coverage. Ideal in houses and office areas.
Directional – Focus the radio signal in a specific direction. Examples are the Yagi and parabolic dish.
Multiple Input Multiple Output (MIMO) – Uses multiple antennas (Up to eight) to increase bandwidth.

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Top Level\_imgs_wlan\slide20_shape2.png
images.slide_text:
WLAN Operation802.11 Wireless Topology Modes
Ad hoc mode - Used to connect clients in peer-to-peer manner without an AP.

Infrastructure mode - Used to connect clients to the network using an AP.

Tethering - Variation of the ad hoc topology is when a smart phone or tablet with cellular data access is enabled to create a personal hotspot.

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Top Level\_imgs_wlan\slide20_shape3.png
images.slide_text:

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Top Level\_imgs_wlan\slide21_shape2.png
images.slide_text:
WLAN OperationBSS and ESS
Infrastructure mode defines two topology blocks:
Basic Service Set (BSS)
Uses single AP to interconnect all associated wireless clients.
Clients in different BSSs cannot communicate.
Extended Service Set (ESS)
A union of two or more BSSs interconnected by a wired distribution system.
Clients in each BSS can communication through the ESS.

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Top Level\_imgs_wlan\slide21_shape3.png

images.slide_text:
WLAN Operation802.11 Frame Structure
The 802.11 frame format is similar to the Ethernet frame format, except that it contains more fields.

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Top Level\_imgs_wlan\slide24_shape2.png
images.slide_text:
WLAN OperationWireless Client and AP Association
For wireless devices to communicate over a network, they must first associate with an AP or wireless router.
Wireless devices complete the following three stage process:
Discover a wireless AP
Authenticate with the AP
Associate with the AP

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Top Level\_imgs_wlan\slide26_shape2.png
images.slide_text:
WLAN OperationPassive and Active Discover Mode
Wireless clients connect to the AP using a passive or active scanning (probing) process. 
Passive mode – AP openly advertises its service by periodically sending broadcast beacon frames containing the SSID, supported standards, and security settings.
Active mode – Wireless clients must know the name of the SSID. The wireless client initiates the process by broadcasting a probe request frame on multiple channels. 




Passive mode
Active mode

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Top Level\_imgs_wlan\slide26_shape4.png

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Top Level\_imgs_wlan\slide29_shape2.png
images.slide_text:
Channel ManagementChannel Selection
The 2.4 GHz band is subdivided into multiple channels each allotted 22 MHz bandwidth and separated from the next channel by 5 MHz.
A best practice for 802.11b/g/n WLANs requiring multiple APs is to use non-overlapping channels such as 1, 6, and 11.

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Top Level\_imgs_wlan\slide30_shape2.png
images.slide_text:
Channel ManagementChannel Selection (Cont.)
For the 5GHz standards 802.11a/n/ac, there are 24 channels. Each channel is separated from the next channel by 20 MHz.
Non-overlapping channels are 36, 48, and 60.

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Top Level\_imgs_wlan\slide31_shape2.png
images.slide_text:
Channel ManagementPlan a WLAN Deployment
The number of users supported by a WLAN depends on the following:
The geographical layout of the facility
The number of bodies and devices that can fit in a space
The data rates users expect
The use of non-overlapping channels by multiple APs and transmit power settings
When planning the location of APs, the approximate circular coverage area is important.

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Top Level\_imgs_advance_topics\slide2_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
Traditional (Legacy) Networking
Since the explosive growth of the Internet in the 1990s, the networking industry has been dominated by closed and proprietary hardware and software
The interface between control and data planes has been historically proprietary
Vendor dependence: slow product cycles of vendor equipment, no innovation from network owners
A router is a monolithic unit built and internally accessed by the manufacturer only


2

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Top Level\_imgs_advance_topics\slide3_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
SDN
Protocol ossification has been challenged first by SDN
SDN (1) explicitly separates the control and data planes, and (2) enables the control plane intelligence to be implemented as a software outside the switches
The function of populating the forwarding table is now performed by the controller



3

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Top Level\_imgs_advance_topics\slide4_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
SDN Limitation
4
SDN is limited to the OpenFlow specifications 
Forwarding rules are based on a fixed number of protocols / header fields (e.g., IP, Ethernet)
The data plane is designed with fixed functions (hard-coded)
Functions are implemented by the chip designer

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Top Level\_imgs_advance_topics\slide10_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
P4 Programmable Switches
10
P41 programmable switches permit a programmer to program the data plane 
Define and parse new protocols
Customize packet processing functions
Measure events occurring in the data plane with 
      high precision
Offload applications to the data plane

Reproduced from N. McKeown. Creating an End-to-End Programming Model for Packet Forwarding. Available: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fiBuao6YZl0&t=4216s

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Top Level\_imgs_advance_topics\slide12_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
Processor Performance
12
Moore’s law predicted that the number of transistors per chip would double every year
Robert Dennard noted that power density was constant for a given silicon area even as the number of transistors increased 
In 2003, the limits of power due to the end of Dennard Scaling slowed processor performance to 23%
This observation forced the industry to use multiple processors per chip, referred to as cores

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Top Level\_imgs_advance_topics\slide13_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
Processor Performance
13
Multi-core processors have slowed down because of Amdahl’s law
Moore’s law has recently ended!

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Top Level\_imgs_advance_topics\slide14_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
Processor Performance
14
Multi-core processors have slowed down because of Amdahl’s law
Moore’s law has recently ended!
Data volumes keep increasing
Network speeds are also increasing to keep up with the surge of data
There is a need to accelerate data processing!

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Top Level\_imgs_advance_topics\slide16_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
Evolution of Network Interface Cards (NICs)
16
Network Interface Cards (NICs) have evolved over the years
Traditional NICs use fixed-function components to implement basic physical and data-link layer services

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Top Level\_imgs_advance_topics\slide17_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
Evolution of Network Interface Cards (NICs)
17
Network Interface Cards (NICs) have evolved over the years
Traditional NICs use fixed-function components to implement basic physical and data-link layer services
Offload NICs use fixed-function components to offload basic infrastructure functions
Computing IP checksums, encapsulating/de-encapsulating segments, etc.

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Top Level\_imgs_advance_topics\slide18_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
Evolution of Network Interface Cards (NICs)
18
SmartNICs use domain-specific processors to customize packet processing
Programmable packet processing pipeline, regular expression, encryption/decryption, etc.
The domain-specific processors are typically ASIC or FPGA-based
SmartNICs also include general-purpose CPU cores for managing the system

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Top Level\_imgs_advance_topics\slide22_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
What are Anomalies
22
Although anomalies or outliers occur very infrequently, their impact is quite high compared to other events, making their detection extremely important

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Top Level\_imgs_advance_topics\slide25_shape2.png
images.slide_text:
Artificial Neural Networks
Information is processed in units that mimic neurons. 
Multi-Layer Perceptron: Consists of an input layer including a set of sensory nodes as input nodes, one or more hidden layers of computation nodes and an output layer. 
Each interconnection has a scalar weight associated with it that is calculated during the training phase.

images.slide_text:
Intrusion Detection System 
Intruders (worms and viruses) spread in seconds.
A network must immediately recognize and mitigate them.
Firewalls can’t protect against malware/zero-day attacks.
A zero-day attack is an attack that exploits a previously unknown vulnerability

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Top Level\_imgs_Network Security\slide27_shape1.png
images.slide_text:
IDSs passively monitor traffic.
IDS-enabled device copies the traffic stream.
IDS analyzes the copied traffic offline (promiscuous mode).
It compares the captured traffic stream with known malicious signatures.
IDS does not negatively affect the actual packet flow.
Intrusion Detection System

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Top Level\_imgs_Network Security\slide28_shape1.png
images.slide_text:
IDS cannot stop malicious single-packet attacks from reaching the target before responding to the attack.
IDS often requires assistance from other networking devices, such as routers and firewalls, to respond to an attack.
Intrusion Detection System

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Top Level\_imgs_Network Security\slide29_shape1.png
images.slide_text:
An IPS device is implemented in inline mode. 
All ingress & egress traffic must flow through it for processing. 
All packets entering the trusted network must be analyzed. 
L3, L4, and packet payload is analyzed (L2-7). 
IPS can stop single-packet attacks. 
Poorly configured IPS can negatively affect packet flow
Intrusion Prevention System

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Top Level\_imgs_Network Security\slide31_shape1.png
images.slide_text: IDS vs IPS

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Top Level\_imgs_Network Security\slide36_shape2.png
images.slide_text:
Perimeter Security
Perimeter-based network security models date back to early mainframe era
Large mainframe computers located in physically secure “machine rooms”
Perimeter-based physical security is still useful but may not be sufficient

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Top Level\_imgs_Network Security\slide37_shape1.png
images.slide_text:
Today’s data centers are the modern equivalent of machine rooms
Perimeter-based physical security may no longer be sufficient
Microsoft Chicago data center
Perimeter Security

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Top Level\_imgs_Network Security\slide37_shape2.png
images.slide_text:
Today’s data centers are the modern equivalent of machine rooms
Perimeter-based physical security may no longer be sufficient
Microsoft Chicago data center
Perimeter Security

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Top Level\_imgs_Network Security\slide38_shape1.png
images.slide_text:
Today’s data centers are the modern equivalent of machine rooms
Perimeter-based physical security may no longer be sufficient
Deployed at a handful of well-defined ingress and egress points 
It relies on the assumption that everything on internal network is trusted
Perimeter Security

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Top Level\_imgs_Network Security\slide39_shape1.png
images.slide_text:
Remote employees, mobile users, cloud computing solutions blur the distinction between “internal” and “external”
Insiders, whether intentionally malicious or just careless, may present a very real security threat
The potential for sophisticated cyberthreats to penetrate perimeter defenses, in which case they would then have free passage on the internal network
Perimeter Security

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 1 - Basic device configuration\_imgs_IOS-review\slide26_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
Console Password
<number>
The console port of network devices must be secured




The command line console 0 is used to enter line configuration mode for the console
The zero is used to represent the first (and in most cases only) console interface
The second command, password cisco specifies a password for the console line
The login command configures the switch to require authentication upon login

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 1 - Basic device configuration\_imgs_IOS-review\slide27_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
Privileged EXEC Access
<number>
To secure privileged EXEC access, use the enable secret password command





The enable secret class command is configured and now privileged EXEC access is secured

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 1 - Basic device configuration\_imgs_IOS-review\slide28_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
VTY Password
<number>
The vty lines allow access to a Cisco device via Telnet
By default, many Cisco switches support up to 16 vty lines that are numbered 0 to 15
However, five is the most common number of vty lines configured, lines numbered 0 to 4 
A password needs to be set for all available vty lines
The same password can be set for all connections

Switch Files
5

IOS image file:  IOS facilitates the basic operation of the device’s hardware components
Startup configuration file: contains commands that are used to initially configure a router 
Running configuration: governs the behavior of the device; modified when the network administrator performs configuration

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 1 - Basic device configuration\_imgs_Module 1 - basic device configuration I\slide7_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
Initial Configuration of a Switch
Boot loader finds the IOS image as follows: 
Switch attempts to automatically boot by using information in the BOOT environment variable
If BOOT is not set, it attempts to load and execute the image by performing a recursive, depth-first search throughout flash system
In a depth-first search of a directory, each encountered subdirectory is completely searched before continuing the search in the original directory
7

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 1 - Basic device configuration\_imgs_Module 1 - basic device configuration I\slide8_shape2.png
images.slide_text:
Initial Configuration of a Switch
For remote management access, the switch must be configured with an IP address, mask, and default gateway
Similar to configuring the IP address on host devices
A switch virtual interface (SVI) should be assigned an IP address; the SVI is a virtual interface, not a physical port
8

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 1 - Basic device configuration\_imgs_Module 1 - basic device configuration I\slide9_shape2.png
images.slide_text:
Initial Configuration of a Switch
SVI is a concept related to VLANs
VLANs are logical groups to which ports can be assigned
By default, the switch is configured to have the management of the switch controlled through VLAN 1
All ports are assigned to VLAN 1 by default
9

images.slide_text:
Console Password
<number>
The console port of network devices must be secured




The command line console 0 is used to enter line configuration mode for the console
The zero is used to represent the first (and in most cases only) console interface
The second command, password cisco specifies a password for the console line
The login command configures the switch to require authentication upon login

VTY Password
<number>
The vty lines allow access to a Cisco device via Telnet
By default, many Cisco switches support up to 16 vty lines that are numbered 0 to 15
However, five is the most common number of vty lines configured, lines numbered 0 to 4 
A password needs to be set for all available vty lines
The same password can be set for all connections

images.slide_text:
Unicast Transmission
<number>
Verification
In an IPv4 network, the hosts can communicate one of three different ways:
Unicast, Broadcast, and Multicast
#1 Unicast – the process of sending a packet from one host to an individual host.

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 1 - Basic device configuration\_imgs_review_IP_Addressing\slide19_shape5.png
images.slide_text:
Unicast Transmission
<number>
Verification
In an IPv4 network, the hosts can communicate one of three different ways:
Unicast, Broadcast, and Multicast
#2 Broadcast – the process of sending a packet from one host to all hosts in the network. 
NOTE: Routers do not forward a limited broadcast!
Directed broadcast
Destination 172.16.4.255 
Hosts within the 172.16.4.0/24 network

Assignment of IP Addresses
<number>
Verification

Tier 2 ISPs generally focus on business customers.
Tier 3 ISPs purchase their Internet service from Tier 2 ISPs.
Tier 3 ISPs often bundle Internet connectivity as a part of network and computer service contracts for their customers.
ISPs are large national or international ISPs that are directly connected to the Internet backbone.

IP Version 6
<number>
Verification
There is not a single date to move to IPv6. For the foreseeable future, both IPv4 and IPv6 will coexist
Migration techniques
Dual stack 
Dual-stack: Allows IPv4 and IPv6 to coexist on the same network. Devices run both IPv4 and IPv6 protocol stacks simultaneously.

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 1 - Basic device configuration\_imgs_review_IP_Addressing\slide31_shape5.png
images.slide_text:
IP Version 6
<number>
Verification
There is not a single date to move to IPv6. For the foreseeable future, both IPv4 and IPv6 will coexist
Migration techniques
Dual stack
Tunneling 
Tunneling:  A method of transporting an IPv6 packet over an IPv4 network. The IPv6 packet is encapsulated inside an IPv4 packet.

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 1 - Basic device configuration\_imgs_review_IP_Addressing\slide32_shape5.png
images.slide_text:
IP Version 6
<number>
Verification
There is not a single date to move to IPv6. For the foreseeable future, both IPv4 and IPv6 will coexist
Migration techniques
Dual stack
Tunneling
Translation
Translation: The Network Address Translation 64 (NAT64) allows IPv6-enabled devices to communicate with IPv4-enabled devices using a translation technique. An IPv6 packet is translated to an IPv4 packet, and vice versa.

IPv6 Address Representation
<number>
Verification

Rules to reduce the notation of IPv6 addresses
Omit any leading 0s (zeros)

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 1 - Basic device configuration\_imgs_review_IP_Addressing\slide38_shape5.png
images.slide_text:
IPv6 Address Representation
<number>
Verification

Rules to reduce the notation of IPv6 addresses
Omit any leading 0s (zeros)
A double colon (::) can replace any single, contiguous string of one or more 16-bit segments (hextets) consisting of all zeros. Double colon (::) can only be used once

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 1 - Basic device configuration\_imgs_review_IP_Addressing\slide39_shape5.png

<number>

Rules to reduce the notation of IPv6 addresses
Omit any leading 0s (zeros)
A double colon (::) can replace any single, contiguous string of one or more 16-bit segments (hextets) consisting of all zeros. Double colon (::) can only be used once

images.slide_text:
IPv6 Prefix Length
<number>
Verification

IPv6 does not use the dotted-decimal subnet mask notation
Prefix length indicates the network portion of an IPv6 address using the following format: 
IPv6 address/prefix length
Prefix length can range from 0 to 128
Typical prefix length is /64
Example of /64 prefix:

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 1 - Basic device configuration\_imgs_review_IP_Addressing\slide42_shape5.png
images.slide_text:
IPv6 Address Types
<number>
Verification

Unicast  
Uniquely identifies an interface on an IPv6-enabled device.
A packet sent to a unicast address is received by the interface that is assigned that address.

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 1 - Basic device configuration\_imgs_review_IP_Addressing\slide43_shape4.png

images.slide_text:
IPv6 Unicast Link Local Address
<number>
Verification

Every IPv6-enabled network interface is REQUIRED to have a link-local address
Enables a device to communicate with other IPv6-enabled devices on the same link and only on that link (subnet)
FE80::/10 range, first 10 bits are 1111 1110 10xx xxxx
1111 1110 1000 0000 (FE80) - 1111 1110 1011 1111 (FEBF)

<number>

Packets with a source or destination link-local address cannot be routed beyond the link from where the packet originated

Console Password
<number>
The console port of network devices must be secured




The command line console 0 is used to enter line configuration mode for the console
The zero is used to represent the first (and in most cases only) console interface
The second command, password cisco specifies a password for the console line
The login command configures the switch to require authentication upon login

A password needs to be set for all available vty lines
The same password can be set for all connections

#2 Broadcast – the process of sending a packet from one host to all hosts in the network. 
NOTE: Routers do not forward a limited broadcast!
Directed broadcast
Destination 172.16.4.255 
Hosts within the 172.16.4.0/24 network

ISPs are large national or international ISPs that are directly connected to the Internet backbone.

There is not a single date to move to IPv6. For the foreseeable future, both IPv4 and IPv6 will coexist

Dual-stack: Allows IPv4 and IPv6 to coexist on the same network. Devices run both IPv4 and IPv6 protocol stacks simultaneously.

Tunneling:  A method of transporting an IPv6 packet over an IPv4 network. The IPv6 packet is encapsulated inside an IPv4 packet.

<number>
Verification
There is not a single date to move to IPv6. For the foreseeable future, both IPv4 and IPv6 will coexist
Migration techniques
Dual stack
Tunneling
Translation
Translation: The Network Address Translation 64 (NAT64) allows IPv6-enabled devices to communicate with IPv4-enabled devices using a translation technique. An IPv6 packet is translated to an IPv4 packet, and vice versa.

images.slide_text:
Collision Domains
2
The network segments that share the same bandwidth between devices are known as collision domains
In hub-based Ethernet, devices compete for the medium
Devices must take turns when transmitting
When two or more devices within that segment try to communicate at the same time, collisions may occur

images.slide_text:
Store-and-forward
19
Store-and-forward method forwards a frame after it has received the entire frame and then checked the frame for errors
The switch drops frames that do not pass the FCS check
The ingress port buffering process enables switches to support any mix of Ethernet speeds 
E.g., an incoming frame received from a 100 Mbps ingress port that must be sent out a 1 Gbps egress port requires store-and-forward method

images.slide_text:
Cut-through
20
Cut-through begins the forwarding process after the destination MAC address of an incoming frame and the egress port are determined
The cut-through switching method may forward invalid frames because no FCS check is performed

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 2 - Switching Concepts\_imgs_Module 2 - Switching Concepts Complete\slide19_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
Store-and-forward
19
Store-and-forward method forwards a frame after it has received the entire frame and then checked the frame for errors
The switch drops frames that do not pass the FCS check
The ingress port buffering process enables switches to support any mix of Ethernet speeds 
E.g., an incoming frame received from a 100 Mbps ingress port that must be sent out a 1 Gbps egress port requires store-and-forward method

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 2 - Switching Concepts\_imgs_Module 2 - Switching Concepts Complete\slide20_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
Cut-through
20
Cut-through begins the forwarding process after the destination MAC address of an incoming frame and the egress port are determined
The cut-through switching method may forward invalid frames because no FCS check is performed

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 2 - Switching Concepts\_imgs_Module 2 - Switching Concepts Complete\slide21_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
Collision Domains
21
The network segments that share the same bandwidth between devices are known as collision domains
In hub-based Ethernet, devices compete for the medium
Devices must take turns when transmitting
When two or more devices within that segment try to communicate at the same time, collisions may occur

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 2 - Switching Concepts\_imgs_Module 2 - Switching Concepts Complete\slide22_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
Collision Domains
22
The network segments that share the same bandwidth between devices are known as collision domains
In hub-based Ethernet, devices compete for the medium
Devices must take turns when transmitting
When two or more devices within that segment try to communicate at the same time, collisions may occur

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 2 - Switching Concepts\_imgs_Module 2 - Switching Concepts Complete\slide24_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
Broadcast Domains
24
A collection of connected switches forms a single broadcast domain
When a device sends a Layer 2 broadcast, the destination MAC address in the frame is set to all binary ones
A broadcast frame is received by all devices in the broadcast domain
The router becomes the boundary of a broadcast domain

pptx_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 3 - Virtual Local Area Networks/Lecture 1 - vlans fundamentals 1.ppt

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 3 - Virtual Local Area Networks\_imgs_Lecture 1 - vlans fundamentals 1\slide3_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
VLAN Concept
<number>
VLAN concept: allow a manager to configure a single switch to emulate multiple, independent switches
A manager specifies a set of ports on the switch and designates them to be on virtual LAN 1, virtual LAN 2, etc.
A computer on virtual LAN 18 broadcasts a packet, only those computers on the same virtual LAN receive the packet

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 3 - Virtual Local Area Networks\_imgs_Lecture 1 - vlans fundamentals 1\slide4_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
Benefits of VLANs
<number>
Improved security (separation) and efficiency (shrinking broadcast domain)
Reduced cost (less equipment / bandwidth)
Simpler management (separated functions per VLAN)
VLANs enable the implementation of access and security policies according to specific groups

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 3 - Virtual Local Area Networks\_imgs_Lecture 1 - vlans fundamentals 1\slide5_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
Benefits of VLANs
<number>
Each VLAN is an IP network
VLAN design must consider the implementation of a hierarchical network-addressing scheme

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 3 - Virtual Local Area Networks\_imgs_Lecture 1 - vlans fundamentals 1\slide8_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
VLAN Classification
<number>
All ports become a part of default VLAN after initial boot
All ports in the same broadcast domain, allowing any device to communicate with other devices on other device on a different port

pptx_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 3 - Virtual Local Area Networks/Lecture 2 - vlans - trunks and 802-1Q.ppt

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 3 - Virtual Local Area Networks\_imgs_Lecture 2 - vlans - trunks and 802-1Q\slide2_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
Multi-switched Environments – Trunks
<number>
To extend VLANs across an entire network, switches communicate with each other through trunks
A trunk is a point-to-point link between two switches that carries traffic from more than one VLAN

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 3 - Virtual Local Area Networks\_imgs_Lecture 2 - vlans - trunks and 802-1Q\slide3_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
Networks without VLANs
<number>
When receiving a broadcast frame, the switch forwards it through all other ports except the port where the broadcast was received

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 3 - Virtual Local Area Networks\_imgs_Lecture 2 - vlans - trunks and 802-1Q\slide4_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
Networks with VLANs
<number>
When a broadcast frame is sent from a device on a VLAN, the switch forwards that broadcast frame only to those switch ports configured to support that VLAN

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 3 - Virtual Local Area Networks\_imgs_Lecture 2 - vlans - trunks and 802-1Q\slide5_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
Networks with VLANs
<number>
When frames are placed on a trunk, information about the VLANs to which they belong must be added – “tagging”
“Tagging” is accomplished by using IEEE 802.1Q header 
802.1Q Frame

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 3 - Virtual Local Area Networks\_imgs_Lecture 2 - vlans - trunks and 802-1Q\slide5_shape4.png
images.slide_text:

<number>
When frames are placed on a trunk, information about the VLANs to which they belong must be added – “tagging”
“Tagging” is accomplished by using IEEE 802.1Q header 
802.1Q Frame

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 3 - Virtual Local Area Networks\_imgs_Lecture 2 - vlans - trunks and 802-1Q\slide6_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
802.1Q Frame
<number>
Type - protocol ID; for Ethernet, hexadecimal 0x8100
User priority - supports level or service implementation
Canonical Format Identifier (CFI) - enables Token Ring frames to be carried across Ethernet links
VLAN ID (VID) - VLAN
   ID number
802.1Q Frame

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 3 - Virtual Local Area Networks\_imgs_Lecture 2 - vlans - trunks and 802-1Q\slide7_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
Untagged Frames on a Native VLAN
<number>
When a trunk port receives untagged frames (unusual), it forwards to the native VLAN
 The default native VLAN is VLAN 1

<number>
An access port can belong to only one VLAN at a time
one exception is a port connected to an IP phone, in which case, there are two VLANs (one for voice and one for data) associated

images.slide_text:
VLAN Membership Change
<number>
Port reassignment
When an access port is reassigned to another VLAN, the new VLAN membership replaces the previous VLAN membership
Or a membership can simply be removed (without assignment any new VLAN to that port) with no switchport access vlan
E.g., interface F0/18 was previously assigned to VLAN 20

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 3 - Virtual Local Area Networks\_imgs_Lecture 3 - VLAN configuration\slide6_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
Deleting a VLAN
<number>
A VLAN can be removed from a switch








Before deleting a VLAN, be sure to first reassign all member ports to a different VLAN
Otherwise, a non-reassigned port is unable to communicate with other hosts, until they it is assigned to an active VLAN

VLAN Trunks
<number>
A VLAN trunk is an OSI Layer 2 link between two switches
It carries traffic for all VLANs 
unless allowed VLAN list is restricted manually or dynamically
A port must be configured as trunk; switchport mode trunk

<number>
Port reassignment
When an access port is reassigned to another VLAN, the new VLAN membership replaces the previous VLAN membership
Or a membership can simply be removed (without assignment any new VLAN to that port) with no switchport access vlan
E.g., interface F0/18 was previously assigned to VLAN 20

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 3 - Virtual Local Area Networks\_imgs_Lecture 3 - VLAN configuration\slide6_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
Deleting a VLAN
<number>
A VLAN can be removed from a switch








Before deleting a VLAN, be sure to first reassign all member ports to a different VLAN
Otherwise, a non-reassigned port is unable to communicate with other hosts, until they it is assigned to an active VLAN

images.slide_text:
VLAN Trunks
<number>
A VLAN trunk is an OSI Layer 2 link between two switches
It carries traffic for all VLANs 
unless allowed VLAN list is restricted manually or dynamically
A port must be configured as trunk; switchport mode trunk

images.slide_text:
Inter-VLAN Routing
3
Using VLANs improves performance, manageability, and security
However, because VLANs segment the network, a L3 process is required to move packets among segments
L3 routing can be implemented w/ a router or a L3 switch
L3 devices control the traffic between segments

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 4 - InterVLAN Routing\_imgs_Lecture 1 - Intro to InterVLAN and Legacy InterVLAN\slide4_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
Inter-VLAN Routing
4
VLANs are used to segment switched networks
L2 switches have limited IPv4/IPv6 functionality and cannot perform routing functions
Devices on separate VLANs are unable to communicate without the intervention of a routing device
Forwarding traffic from one VLAN to another VLAN using routing is known as inter-VLAN routing

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 4 - InterVLAN Routing\_imgs_Lecture 1 - Intro to InterVLAN and Legacy InterVLAN\slide5_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
Legacy Inter-VLAN Routing
5
Historically, the first solution for inter-VLAN routing relied on routers with multiple physical interfaces
Switch ports connected to the router are placed in access mode; each interface is assigned to a different VLAN

images.slide_text:
Router-on-a-Stick Inter-VLAN Routing
3
Only one physical interface is required on the router and the switch to route packets between multiple VLANs
“Router-on-a-stick” is router behavior where a single interface routes traffic among VLANs
The router interface operates as a trunk link and is connected to a switch port operating in trunk mode

fixed_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 4 - InterVLAN Routing/Lecture 3 - L3 switches and Inter-VLAN_fixed.pptx

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 4 - InterVLAN Routing\_imgs_Lecture 3 - L3 switches and Inter-VLAN\slide5_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
L3 Switch Inter-VLAN Routing
5
A Switch Virtual Interface (SVI) is a virtual interface 
There is no physical port dedicated to the interface
An SVI can be created for any VLAN on the switch
The SVI can perform the same functions for the VLAN as a router interface would

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 4 - InterVLAN Routing\_imgs_Lecture 3 - L3 switches and Inter-VLAN\slide6_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
L3 Switch Inter-VLAN Routing
6
A routed port is a physical port that acts as an interface on a router (however, it does not support subinterfaces)
A routed port is not associated with a particular VLAN
Because L2 functionality is removed, L2 protocols, such as STP, do not function on a routed interface 
Routed ports are used for 
  point-to-point links
Routed ports are mostly 
   configured between switches 
   in the core and distribution layer

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 4 - InterVLAN Routing\_imgs_Lecture 3 - L3 switches and Inter-VLAN\slide7_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
Practical Observations Layer 3 Switches
7
Consider the figure below:
VLANs are created as in layer-2 switches
Assignment of VLANs to access ports is done as in layer-2 switches
To enable routing:
	Layer3(config)#ip routing
To make G0/1 a trunk:
	Layer3(config-if)#switchport trunk encapsulation dot1q
	Layer3(config-if)#switchport mode trunk
Assignment of IP addresses to SVIs is 
  done as in layer-2 switches

images.slide_text:
L3 Switch Inter-VLAN Routing
<number>
Most enterprise networks use L3 switches to achieve high processing rates using hardware-based switching
L3 switches usually have packet-switching throughputs in the millions of packets per second (pps)

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 4 - InterVLAN Routing\_imgs_Module4-interVLAN\slide12_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
L3 Switch Inter-VLAN Routing
<number>
As network technologies have evolved, routing has become faster and cheaper
Today, routing can be performed at hardware speed
Routing now can be transferred to the core and the distribution layers without 
   impacting network 
   performance

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 4 - InterVLAN Routing\_imgs_Module4-interVLAN\slide13_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
L3 Switch Inter-VLAN Routing
<number>
Each VLAN is usually a separate subnet
It is logical to configure the distribution switches as L3 gateways for the users of each access switch
L3 (routed) ports are normally implemented between distribution and core layers
The network architecture 
   is not dependent on 
   spanning tree, because 
   there are no physical 
   loops at L2

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 4 - InterVLAN Routing\_imgs_Module4-interVLAN\slide14_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
L3 Switch Inter-VLAN Routing
<number>
A Switch Virtual Interface (SVI) is a virtual interface 
There is no physical port dedicated to the interface
An SVI can be created for any VLAN on the switch
It can perform the same functions for the VLAN as a router interface would
The SVI for the VLAN provides L3 processing for packets to or from all switch ports associated with that VLAN

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 4 - InterVLAN Routing\_imgs_Module4-interVLAN\slide15_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
L3 Switch Inter-VLAN Routing
<number>
A routed port is a physical port that acts as an interface on a router (however, it does not support subinterfaces)
A routed port is not associated with a particular VLAN
Because L2 functionality is removed, L2 protocols, such as STP, do not function on a routed interface 
Routed ports are used for 
  point-to-point links
Routed ports are mostly 
   configured between switches 
   in the core and distribution layer

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 4 - InterVLAN Routing\_imgs_Module4-interVLAN\slide16_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
Troubleshooting L3 Switching
<number>
The following items should be checked
VLANs must be defined across all the switches. VLANs must be enabled on the trunk ports. Ports must be in the right VLANs
SVIs must have the correct IP address or subnet mask. SVI must be up. SVI must match with the VLAN number
Routing must be enabled. Each interface or network should be added to the routing protocol
Hosts must have the correct IP 
   address or subnet mask. Hosts must
   have a default gateway associate

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 4 - InterVLAN Routing\_imgs_Module4-interVLAN\slide17_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
Practical Observations Layer 3 Switches
<number>
Consider the figure below:
VLANs are created as in layer-2 switches
Assignment of VLANs to access ports is done as in layer-2 switches
To enable routing:
	Layer3(config)#ip routing
To make G0/1 a trunk:
	Layer3(config-if)#switchport trunk encapsulation dot1q
	Layer3(config-if)#switchport mode trunk
Assignment of IP addresses to SVIs is 
  done as in layer-2 switches

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 4 - InterVLAN Routing\_imgs_Module4-interVLAN\slide3_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
Inter-VLAN Routing
<number>
The use of VLANs improved performance, manageability, and security
However, because VLANs segment the network, a L3 process is required to move packets among segments
L3 routing can be implemented w/ a router or a L3 switch
L3 devices control the traffic flow between segments

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 4 - InterVLAN Routing\_imgs_Module4-interVLAN\slide4_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
Inter-VLAN Routing
<number>
VLANs are used to segment switched networks
L2 switches have limited IPv4/IPv6 functionality and cannot perform routing functions
Devices on separate VLANs are unable to communicate without the intervention of a routing device
Forwarding traffic from one VLAN to another VLAN using routing is known as inter-VLAN routing

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 4 - InterVLAN Routing\_imgs_Module4-interVLAN\slide5_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
Legacy Inter-VLAN Routing
<number>
Historically, the first solution for inter-VLAN routing relied on routers with multiple physical interfaces
Switch ports connected to the router are placed in access mode; each interface is assigned to a different VLAN

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 4 - InterVLAN Routing\_imgs_Module4-interVLAN\slide6_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
Legacy Inter-VLAN Routing
<number>
Historically, the first solution for inter-VLAN routing relied on routers with multiple physical interfaces
Switch ports connected to the router are placed in access mode; each interface is assigned to a different VLAN

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 4 - InterVLAN Routing\_imgs_Module4-interVLAN\slide6_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
Legacy Inter-VLAN Routing
<number>
Historically, the first solution for inter-VLAN routing relied on routers with multiple physical interfaces
Switch ports connected to the router are placed in access mode; each interface is assigned to a different VLAN

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 4 - InterVLAN Routing\_imgs_Module4-interVLAN\slide7_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
Router-on-a-Stick Inter-VLAN Routing
<number>
Only one physical interface is required on the router and the switch to route packets between multiple VLANs
“Router-on-a-stick” is router behavior where a single interface routes traffic among VLANs
The router interface operates as a trunk link and is connected to a switch port operating in trunk mode

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 4 - InterVLAN Routing\_imgs_Module4-interVLAN\slide8_shape3.png
images.slide_text:

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 4 - InterVLAN Routing\_imgs_Module4-interVLAN\slide10_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
L3 Switch Inter-VLAN Routing
<number>
Most enterprise networks use L3 switches to achieve high processing rates using hardware-based switching
L3 switches usually have packet-switching throughputs in the millions of packets per second (pps)

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 4 - InterVLAN Routing\_imgs_Module4-interVLAN\slide12_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
L3 Switch Inter-VLAN Routing
<number>
As network technologies have evolved, routing has become faster and cheaper
Today, routing can be performed at hardware speed
Routing now can be transferred to the core and the distribution layers without 
   impacting network 
   performance

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 4 - InterVLAN Routing\_imgs_Module4-interVLAN\slide13_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
L3 Switch Inter-VLAN Routing
<number>
Each VLAN is usually a separate subnet
It is logical to configure the distribution switches as L3 gateways for the users of each access switch
L3 (routed) ports are normally implemented between distribution and core layers
The network architecture 
   is not dependent on 
   spanning tree, because 
   there are no physical 
   loops at L2

It can perform the same functions for the VLAN as a router interface would
The SVI for the VLAN provides L3 processing for packets to or from all switch ports associated with that VLAN

pptx_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 5 - Spanning Tree Protocol/Lecture 2 - Building the Spanning Tree.pptx
fixed_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 5 - Spanning Tree Protocol/Lecture 2 - Building the Spanning Tree_fixed.pptx

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 5 - Spanning Tree Protocol\_imgs_Lecture 2 - Building the Spanning Tree\slide4_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
1. Elect the Root Bridge
4
After a switch boots, it sends out BPDU frames every two seconds
BPDUs contain the BID of the sending switch and the BID of the root bridge, known as the Root ID
The switch with the lowest BID will become the root bridge
At first, all switches declare themselves as the root bridge with their own BID set as the Root ID
Eventually, the switches learn through the exchange of BPDUs which switch has the lowest BID and will agree on one root bridge

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 5 - Spanning Tree Protocol\_imgs_Lecture 2 - Building the Spanning Tree\slide5_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
1. Elect the Root Bridge
5
Because the default BID is 32768, it is possible for two or more switches to have the same priority
In this scenario, where the priorities are the same, the switch with the lowest MAC address will become the root bridge

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 5 - Spanning Tree Protocol\_imgs_Lecture 2 - Building the Spanning Tree\slide6_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
2a. Determine the Root Path Cost
6
STA starts the process of determining the best paths to the root bridge from all destinations in the broadcast domain
The BPDU includes the root path cost. This is the cost of the path from the sending switch to the root bridge

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 5 - Spanning Tree Protocol\_imgs_Lecture 2 - Building the Spanning Tree\slide7_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
2b. Elect the Root Ports
7
Every non-root switch will select one root port
The root port is the port closest to the root bridge in terms of overall cost (best path) to the root bridge. This cost is known as the internal root path cost

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 5 - Spanning Tree Protocol\_imgs_Lecture 2 - Building the Spanning Tree\slide8_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
3. Elect Designated Ports
8
Every segment between two switches will have one designated port
The designated port has the best path to receive traffic leading to the root bridge

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 5 - Spanning Tree Protocol\_imgs_Lecture 2 - Building the Spanning Tree\slide9_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
4. Elect Alternate (Blocked) Ports
9
If a port is not a root port or a designated port, then it becomes an alternate (or backup) port
Alternate ports and backup ports are in discarding or blocking state to prevent loops

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 5 - Spanning Tree Protocol\_imgs_Lecture 2 - Building the Spanning Tree\slide11_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
Elect a Root Port from Multiple Equal-Cost Paths
11
Lowest sender BID
S2 has two ports with equal cost paths to the root bridge
The BIDs of the neighboring switches will be used to break the tie
S3 has a BID of 32769.5555.5555.5555 and S4 has a BID of 32769.1111.1111.1111. Because S4 has a lower BID, the F0/1 port of S2, which is the port connected to S4, will be the root port

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 5 - Spanning Tree Protocol\_imgs_Lecture 2 - Building the Spanning Tree\slide12_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
Elect a Root Port from Multiple Equal-Cost Paths
12
Lowest Sender Port ID
Switch S4 has received BPDUs from port F0/1 and port F0/2 on S1. Remember the decision is based on the sender’s port ID, not the receiver’s port ID
Because the port ID of F0/1 on S1 is lower than port F0/2, the port F0/6 on switch S4 will be the root port

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 5 - Spanning Tree Protocol\_imgs_Lecture 2 - Building the Spanning Tree\slide13_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
STP Timers and Port States
13
STP convergence requires three timers, as follows:
Hello Timer -The hello time is the interval between BPDUs. The default is 2 seconds but can be modified to between 1 and 10 seconds
Forward Delay Timer -The forward delay is the time that is spent in the listening and learning state. The default is 15 seconds but can be modified to between 4 and 30 seconds.
Max Age Timer -The max age is the maximum length of time that a switch waits before attempting to change the S

images.slide_text:
STP Timers and Port States
14

images.slide_text:
STP Timers and Port States
14

fixed_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 7 - DHCPv4/Introduction to DHCP_fixed.pptx

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 7 - DHCPv4\_imgs_Introduction to DHCP\slide4_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
A dedicated DHCPv4 server is scalable and relatively easy to manage. 
The DHCPv4 server dynamically assigns, or leases, an IPv4 address from a pool of addresses for a limited time.
Administrators configure DHCPv4 servers to set the leases to time out at different intervals. 
When the lease expires, the client must ask for another address, although the client is typically reassigned the same address.
4
DHCPv4 Concepts

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 7 - DHCPv4\_imgs_Introduction to DHCP\slide5_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
The steps to obtain a lease are:
DHCP Discover (DHCPDISCOVER)
DHCP Offer (DHCPOFFER)
DHCP Request (DHCPREQUEST)
DHCP Acknowledgment (DHCPACK)
5
DHCPv4: Obtain a Lease

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 7 - DHCPv4\_imgs_Introduction to DHCP\slide6_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
The steps to obtain a lease are:
DHCP Discover (DHCPDISCOVER)
DHCP Offer (DHCPOFFER)
DHCP Request (DHCPREQUEST)
DHCP Acknowledgment (DHCPACK)
6
DHCPv4: Obtain a Lease
Note: These messages (primarily the DHCPOFFER and DHCPACK) can be sent as unicast or broadcast according to IETF RFC 2131.

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 7 - DHCPv4\_imgs_Introduction to DHCP\slide7_shape5.png
images.slide_text:
Prior to lease expiration, the client begins a two-step process to renew the lease with the DHCPv4 server, as shown in the figure:
DHCP Request (DHCPREQUEST)
DHCP Acknowledgment (DHCPACK)
7
DHCPv4: Renew a Lease
Note: These messages (primarily the DHCPOFFER and DHCPACK) can be sent as unicast or broadcast according to IETF RFC 2131.

In a complex network, enterprise servers are usually located centrally. 
These servers may provide DHCP, DNS, TFTP, and FTP services. 
Network clients are not typically on the same subnet as those servers. 
To locate the servers and receive services, clients often use broadcast messages.
16

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 7 - DHCPv4\_imgs_Introduction to DHCP\slide4_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
A dedicated DHCPv4 server is scalable and relatively easy to manage. 
The DHCPv4 server dynamically assigns, or leases, an IPv4 address from a pool of addresses for a limited time.
Administrators configure DHCPv4 servers to set the leases to time out at different intervals. 
When the lease expires, the client must ask for another address, although the client is typically reassigned the same address.
4
DHCPv4 Concepts

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 7 - DHCPv4\_imgs_Introduction to DHCP\slide5_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
The steps to obtain a lease are:
DHCP Discover (DHCPDISCOVER)
DHCP Offer (DHCPOFFER)
DHCP Request (DHCPREQUEST)
DHCP Acknowledgment (DHCPACK)
5
DHCPv4: Obtain a Lease

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 7 - DHCPv4\_imgs_Introduction to DHCP\slide6_shape4.png
images.slide_text:

DHCP Discover (DHCPDISCOVER)
DHCP Offer (DHCPOFFER)
DHCP Request (DHCPREQUEST)
DHCP Acknowledgment (DHCPACK)
6
DHCPv4: Obtain a Lease
Note: These messages (primarily the DHCPOFFER and DHCPACK) can be sent as unicast or broadcast according to IETF RFC 2131.

images.slide_text:
Prior to lease expiration, the client begins a two-step process to renew the lease with the DHCPv4 server, as shown in the figure:
DHCP Request (DHCPREQUEST)
DHCP Acknowledgment (DHCPACK)
7

Note: These messages (primarily the DHCPOFFER and DHCPACK) can be sent as unicast or broadcast according to IETF RFC 2131.

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 8 - SLAAC and DHCPv6\_imgs_Lecture 1 IPv6 Global Unicast Address Assignment\slide4_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
Link local 
Used to communicate with other devices on the same local link (same LAN)
Every IPv6-enabled network interface is REQUIRED to have a link-local address
Link local addresses are in the range FE80::/10; the first 10 bits are 1111 1110 10
The first hextet will be between 1111 1110 1000 0000 (FE80) – 1111 1110 1011 1111 (FEBF)


4
Review – IPv6 Addresses

images.slide_text:
A host can be manually configured with an IPv6 GUA address
However, manually entering an IPv6 GUA can be time consuming and somewhat error prone
Therefore, hosts are enabled to dynamically acquire an IPv6 GUA configuration


5
IPv6 Host Configuration
Static configuration
Dynamic configuration

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 8 - SLAAC and DHCPv6\_imgs_Lecture 1 IPv6 Global Unicast Address Assignment\slide5_shape5.png
images.slide_text:
A host can be manually configured with an IPv6 GUA address
However, manually entering an IPv6 GUA can be time consuming and somewhat error prone
Therefore, hosts are enabled to dynamically acquire an IPv6 GUA configuration


5
IPv6 Host Configuration
Static configuration
Dynamic configuration

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 8 - SLAAC and DHCPv6\_imgs_Lecture 1 IPv6 Global Unicast Address Assignment\slide7_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
IPv6 was designed to simplify how a host can acquire its IPv6 configuration
By default, an IPv6-enabled router advertises its IPv6 information, which allows a host to dynamically create or acquire its IPv6 configuration
The IPv6 GUA can be assigned dynamically using stateless and stateful services
Stateless and stateful methods use ICMPv6 Router Advertisement (RA) messages to suggest to the host how to create or acquire its IPv6 configuration

7
IPv6 GUA Assignment

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 8 - SLAAC and DHCPv6\_imgs_Lecture 1 IPv6 Global Unicast Address Assignment\slide8_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
An ICMPv6 RA message includes three flags to identify the dynamic options available to a host, as follows:
A flag - Address Autoconfiguration flag. Use Stateless Address Autoconfiguration (SLAAC) to create an IPv6 GUA
O flag - Other Configuration flag. Other information is available from a stateless DHCPv6 server
M flag - Managed Address Configuration flag. Use a stateful DHCPv6 server to obtain an IPv6 GUA
8
Router Advertisement Message Flags

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 8 - SLAAC and DHCPv6\_imgs_Lecture 1 IPv6 Global Unicast Address Assignment\slide10_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
The IPv6 all-routers group responds to the IPv6 multicast address ff02::2
A router sends RA messages to IPv6 all-nodes multicast address ff02::1 every 200s
The SLAAC only method is enabled by default with the command 
 ipv6 unicast-routing
10
Enabling SLAAC

images.slide_text:
SLAAC enables a host to create its own unique IPv6 GUA without the services of a DHCPv6 server
A host configures its IPv6 address based on the information sent in the RA message
RA messages are sent by an IPv6 router every 200s
A host can also send a Router Solicitation (RS) message requesting that an IPv6-enabled router send the host an RA

14
SLAAC Only Method

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 8 - SLAAC and DHCPv6\_imgs_Lecture 1 IPv6 Global Unicast Address Assignment\slide15_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
A = 1 -> the client creates its own IPv6 GUA using the prefix advertised in the RA
Two methods to creates its own Interface ID: EUI-64 method or randomly
O = 0 and M = 0 -> use the information in the RA message exclusively
The RA includes the prefix, prefix-length, DNS server, MTU, and default gateway information
15
SLAAC Only Method
Note: The "%" and number at the end of the link-local address is known as a Zone ID or Scope ID and is used by the OS to associate the LLA with a specific interface

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 8 - SLAAC and DHCPv6\_imgs_Lecture 1 IPv6 Global Unicast Address Assignment\slide16_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
When a client is configured to obtain its address information automatically, it sends an RS message to the IPv6 all-routers multicast address of ff02::2 (step 1)
The router will send an RA message to all-nodes multicast address (ff02::1) upon receiving the RS message from the client (step 2)
A router will also send RA messages every 200s to all-nodes multicast address

16
ICMPv6 RS Messages

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 8 - SLAAC and DHCPv6\_imgs_Lecture 1 IPv6 Global Unicast Address Assignment\slide18_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
A client typically acquires its 64-bit subnet information from the RA message (most significant 64 bits)
It must still generate the remainder 64-bit interface identifier (ID), using one of the following methods:
Randomly generated by the operating system (e.g., Windows OS)
EUI-64: the client creates an interface ID using its 48-bit MAC address. It inserts the hex value of FFFE in the middle of the address, and flips the seventh bit of the interface ID
18
Client Process to Generate Interface ID

images.slide_text:
Link local 
Used to communicate with other devices on the same local link (same LAN)
Every IPv6-enabled network interface is REQUIRED to have a link-local address
Link local addresses are in the range FE80::/10; the first 10 bits are 1111 1110 10
The first hextet will be between 1111 1110 1000 0000 (FE80) – 1111 1110 1011 1111 (FEBF)


4

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 8 - SLAAC and DHCPv6\_imgs_Lecture 1 IPv6 Global Unicast Address Assignment\slide7_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
IPv6 was designed to simplify how a host can acquire its IPv6 configuration
By default, an IPv6-enabled router advertises its IPv6 information, which allows a host to dynamically create or acquire its IPv6 configuration
The IPv6 GUA can be assigned dynamically using stateless and stateful services
Stateless and stateful methods use ICMPv6 Router Advertisement (RA) messages to suggest to the host how to create or acquire its IPv6 configuration

7
IPv6 GUA Assignment

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 8 - SLAAC and DHCPv6\_imgs_Lecture 1 IPv6 Global Unicast Address Assignment\slide8_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
An ICMPv6 RA message includes three flags to identify the dynamic options available to a host, as follows:
A flag - Address Autoconfiguration flag. Use Stateless Address Autoconfiguration (SLAAC) to create an IPv6 GUA
O flag - Other Configuration flag. Other information is available from a stateless DHCPv6 server
M flag - Managed Address Configuration flag. Use a stateful DHCPv6 server to obtain an IPv6 GUA
8
Router Advertisement Message Flags
