images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 8 - SLAAC and DHCPv6\_imgs_Lecture 2 DHCPv6\slide4_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
4
IPv6 GUA Assignment

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 8 - SLAAC and DHCPv6\_imgs_Lecture 2 DHCPv6\slide6_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
Host sends a Router Solicitation (RS) message to the IPv6 all-routers multicast address (ff02::2)
Router responds with a Router Advertisement (RA) message to the all-nodes multicast address (ff02::1)
The host sends a DHCPv6 SOLICIT message to all-DHCPv6-servers address (ff02::1:2)
The DHCPv6 server responds with an ADVERTISE message
The host responds to the DHCPv6 server
The DHCPv6 server sends a REPLY message
6
DHCPv6 Operation Steps

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 8 - SLAAC and DHCPv6\_imgs_Lecture 2 DHCPv6\slide9_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
PC1 receives an RA message including the network prefix and prefix length. A = 1 -> SLAAC; O = 1 -> seek that additional information from a DHCPv6 server
PC1 sends a DHCPv6 SOLICIT message seeking additional information from a stateless DHCPv6 server

9
Stateless DHCPv6

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 8 - SLAAC and DHCPv6\_imgs_Lecture 2 DHCPv6\slide10_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
10
Stateless DHCPv6
Stateless DHCPv6 is enabled on a router interface using the following interface configuration command (this sets the O flag to 1):
 ipv6 nd other-config-flag

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 8 - SLAAC and DHCPv6\_imgs_Lecture 2 DHCPv6\slide13_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
13
Enable Stateful DHCPv6 on an Interface
Stateful DHCPv6 is enabled on a router interface using the following interface configuration commands:
 ipv6 nd managed-config-flag (set M flag to 1)
 ipv6 nd prefix default no-autoconfig (set A flag to 0)

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 10 - LAN Security Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 1 LAN Security Concepts\slide5_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
Various security devices are used to protect the network perimeter from outside access
Next-generation Firewalls (NGFW) provide stateful packet inspection, application visibility, intrusion prevention, advanced malware protection (AMP), URL filtering, and other features
VPN-enabled routers or firewalls provide a secure connection to remote users across a public network
Network Access Control (NAC) devices include authentication, authorization, and accounting (AAA)




5
Perimeter Security

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 10 - LAN Security Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 1 LAN Security Concepts\slide6_shape5.png
images.slide_text:
Endpoints include laptops, desktops, servers, phones
Endpoints are susceptible to malware that originate through email or web browsing
Security features include antivirus/antimalware, host-based firewalls, and host-based intrusion prevention systems (HIPSs)
Endpoints are best protected by a combination of NAC, Advanced Malware Protection (AMP) software, email security appliance (ESA), web security appliance (WSA)




6
Endpoint Protection
Web security appliance
Email security appliance

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 10 - LAN Security Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 1 LAN Security Concepts\slide6_shape6.png
images.slide_text:
Endpoints include laptops, desktops, servers, phones
Endpoints are susceptible to malware that originate through email or web browsing
Security features include antivirus/antimalware, host-based firewalls, and host-based intrusion prevention systems (HIPSs)
Endpoints are best protected by a combination of NAC, Advanced Malware Protection (AMP) software, email security appliance (ESA), web security appliance (WSA)




6
Endpoint Protection
Web security appliance
Email security appliance

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 10 - LAN Security Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 1 LAN Security Concepts\slide7_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
Authenticating a user/device is often required in computer systems, to confirm the identity of the user/device - the party is indeed who or what they claim to be
The simplest method of remote access authentication is to configure a login and password combination on console, vty lines, and aux ports




7
Access Control

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 10 - LAN Security Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 1 LAN Security Concepts\slide8_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
SSH is a more secure form of remote access
It requires a username and a password, both encrypted during transmission
The username and password can be authenticated by the local database method
It provides accountability because the username is recorded when a user logs in




8
Access Control

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 10 - LAN Security Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 1 LAN Security Concepts\slide9_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
AAA stands for Authentication, Authorization, and Accounting
The AAA concept is similar to using a credit card
Identifies the user who can use the card
Establishes how much the user can spend
Keeps an account of what items or services the user purchased



9
AAA Components

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 10 - LAN Security Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 1 LAN Security Concepts\slide11_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
Local AAA stores usernames and passwords locally in a network device (e.g., router)
Users authenticate against the local database
Local AAA is ideal for small networks


11
Authentication

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 10 - LAN Security Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 1 LAN Security Concepts\slide12_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
With the server-based method, the router accesses a central AAA server
The AAA server contains the usernames and passwords for all users
The router uses either the Remote Authentication Dial-In User Service (RADIUS) or Terminal Access Controller Access Control System (TACACS+) protocols to communicate with the AAA server

12
Authentication

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 10 - LAN Security Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 1 LAN Security Concepts\slide13_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
Authorization governs what users can and cannot do after they are authenticated
AAA authorization is automatic and does not require users to perform additional steps

13
Authorization

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 10 - LAN Security Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 1 LAN Security Concepts\slide14_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
AAA accounting collects and reports usage data
This data can be used for purposes such as auditing or billing
The collected data might include the start and stop connection times, executed commands, number of packets, and number of bytes
Accounting also provides evidence for when individuals perform malicious acts

14
Accounting

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 10 - LAN Security Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 1 LAN Security Concepts\slide15_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
The IEEE 802.1X standard is a port-based access control and authentication protocol
This protocol restricts unauthorized workstations from connecting to a LAN through publicly accessible switch ports

15
802.1X

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 10 - LAN Security Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 1 LAN Security Concepts\slide16_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
The IEEE 802.1X standard is a port-based access control and authentication protocol
This protocol restricts unauthorized workstations from connecting to a LAN through publicly accessible switch ports

16
802.1X
Client (Supplicant) - This is a device running 802.1X-compliant client software, which is available for wired or wireless devices

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 10 - LAN Security Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 1 LAN Security Concepts\slide17_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
The IEEE 802.1X standard is a port-based access control and authentication protocol
This protocol restricts unauthorized workstations from connecting to a LAN through publicly accessible switch ports

17
802.1X
Switch (Authenticator) –The switch acts as an intermediary between the client and the authentication server. It requests identifying information from the client, verifies that information with the authentication server, and relays a response to the client

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 10 - LAN Security Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 1 LAN Security Concepts\slide18_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
The IEEE 802.1X standard is a port-based access control and authentication protocol
This protocol restricts unauthorized workstations from connecting to a LAN through publicly accessible switch ports

18
802.1X
Authentication server –The server validates the identity of the client and notifies the switch or wireless access point that the client is or is not authorized to access the LAN and switch services

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 10 - LAN Security Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 1 LAN Security Concepts\slide17_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
The IEEE 802.1X standard is a port-based access control and authentication protocol
This protocol restricts unauthorized workstations from connecting to a LAN through publicly accessible switch ports

17
802.1X
Switch (Authenticator) –The switch acts as an intermediary between the client and the authentication server. It requests identifying information from the client, verifies that information with the authentication server, and relays a response to the client

images.slide_text:
The IEEE 802.1X standard is a port-based access control and authentication protocol
This protocol restricts unauthorized workstations from connecting to a LAN through publicly accessible switch ports

18
802.1X
Authentication server –The server validates the identity of the client and notifies the switch or wireless access point that the client is or is not authorized to access the LAN and switch services

images.slide_text:
Networks are typically protected with VPNs, firewalls, intrusion detection, and other devices operating at layer 3 and above
If a threat actor with access to the internal network captured layer 2 frames, then the security implemented on the layers above may be less effective or even useless
The threat actor could cause a lot of damage on the LAN infrastructure

A switch can run out of resources to store new MAC addresses

When this occurs, the switch treats the frame as an unknown unicast and begins to flood all incoming traffic out all ports (broadcast) 
The threat actor can now capture all frames sent on the local LAN

fixed_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 10 - LAN Security Concepts/Lecture 3 Attack Examples_fixed.pptx

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 10 - LAN Security Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 3 Attack Examples\slide3_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
A VLAN hopping attack enables traffic from one VLAN to be seen by another VLAN 
The attacker configures a host to act like a switch to take advantage of the automatic trunking port feature enabled by default on most switch ports
The attacker configures the host to spoof 802.1Q signaling and Dynamic Trunking Protocol (DTP) signaling to trunk with the connecting switch
3
VLAN Hopping Attack

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 10 - LAN Security Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 3 Attack Examples\slide4_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
If successful, the switch establishes a trunk link with the host
The attacker can now access the traffic from all the VLANs
The attacker can send and receive traffic on any VLAN, hopping between VLANs
4
VLAN Hopping Attack

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 10 - LAN Security Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 3 Attack Examples\slide5_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
The attacker sends a double-tagged 802.1Q frame to the switch
The outer header has the VLAN tag of the attacker, which is the same as the native VLAN of the trunk port. For the example below, VLAN 10 is the native
The inner tag is the victim VLAN, in this example, VLAN 20
5
VLAN Double-Tagging Attack

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 10 - LAN Security Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 3 Attack Examples\slide6_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
The first switch sees that the frame is directed for VLAN 10, native
The switch forwards the packet out all VLAN 10 ports after stripping the VLAN 10 tag
The frame is not retagged because it is part of the native VLAN
6
VLAN Double-Tagging Attack

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 10 - LAN Security Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 3 Attack Examples\slide7_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
The second switch sees that the frame is directed for VLAN 20
The second switch sends the frame on to the target or floods it, depending on whether there is an existing MAC address table entry for the target
7
VLAN Double-Tagging Attack

The goal of the DHCP starvation attack is to create a DoS for connecting clients
DHCP starvation attacks require an attack tool such as Gobbler
Gobbler looks the entire scope of leasable IP addresses and tries to lease them all

Wrong default gateway: the rogue server provides an invalid gateway or the IP address of its host to create a man-in-the-middle attack (attacker intercepts the data flow through the network)
Wrong DNS server: the rogue server provides an incorrect DNS server address
Wrong IP address: the rogue server provides an invalid IP address effectively cre

images.slide_text:
Hosts broadcast ARP requests to determine the MAC address of a host with a particular IPv4 address
All hosts on the subnet receive and process the ARP Request
The host with the matching IPv4 address in the ARP Request sends an ARP Reply
13
ARP Attacks

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 10 - LAN Security Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 3 Attack Examples\slide15_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
A host is also allowed to send an unsolicited ARP Request called a “gratuitous ARP”
When a host sends a gratuitous ARP, other hosts on the subnet store the MAC address and IPv4 address contained in the gratuitous ARP in their ARP tables
IP address spoofing is when an attacker hijacks a valid IP address of another device or uses a random IP address

15
ARP Spoofing Attack
The attacker sends two spoofed gratuitous ARP Replies to replace R1 as the default gateway:
 MAC-address CC:CC:CC maps to R1’s

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 10 - LAN Security Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 3 Attack Examples\slide16_shape5.png
images.slide_text:
A host is also allowed to send an unsolicited ARP Request called a “gratuitous ARP”
When a host sends a gratuitous ARP, other hosts on the subnet store the MAC address and IPv4 address contained in the gratuitous ARP in their ARP tables
IP address spoofing is when an attacker hijacks a valid IP address of another device or uses a random IP address

16
ARP Spoofing Attack – Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)
R1 and PC1 remove the correct entry for each other’s MAC address and replace it with PC2’s MAC addr

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 10 - LAN Security Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 3 Attack Examples\slide17_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
MAC address spoofing attacks occur when an attacker alters its MAC address to match another known MAC address of a target host
The attacking host then sends a frame throughout the network with the newly-configured MAC address
When the switch receives the frame, it examines the source MAC address and overwrites the current MAC table entry and assigns the MAC address to the new port
17
Spoofing Attacks

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 10 - LAN Security Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 3 Attack Examples\slide18_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
Attackers can make their hosts appear as root bridges of the spanning tree and therefore capture all traffic for the immediate switched domain
The attacker broadcasts bridge protocol data units (BPDUs) containing configuration and topology changes that will force spanning-tree recalculations
The BPDUs announce a lower bridge priority to be elected as the root bridge
18
Spanning Tree Protocol Attacks

images.slide_text:
Cisco Discovery Protocol (CDP) is a proprietary Layer 2 link discovery protocol
CDP can automatically discover other CDP-enabled devices
CDP information is sent out CDP-enabled ports in a periodic, unencrypted multicast
Information includes IP address, IOS version, platform, capabilities, native VLAN
Although useful for network troubleshooting the information provided by CDP can also
  be used to discover vulnerabilities
19
CDP Reconnaissance
A sample Wireshark capture displaying the contents of

images.slide_text:
To mitigate the exploitation of CDP, limit the use of CDP on devices or ports
For example, disable CDP on edge ports that connect to untrusted devices
20
CDP Reconnaissance
A sample Wireshark capture displaying the contents of a CDP packet. The attacker can identify the IOS software version and other info.

images.slide_text:
Sequence of events during a DHCP attack
12
DHCP Spoofing Attack
Rogue and valid DHCP servers
A legitimate client connects to the network
The legitimate, rogue DHCP servers respond
The rogue offer was received first, accepted
The rogue server acknowledges the request
1
2
3
4

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 10 - LAN Security Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 3 Attack Examples\slide13_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
Hosts broadcast ARP requests to determine the MAC address of a host with a particular IPv4 address
All hosts on the subnet receive and process the ARP Request
The host with the matching IPv4 address in the ARP Request sends an ARP Reply
13
ARP Attacks

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 10 - LAN Security Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 3 Attack Examples\slide15_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
A host is also allowed to send an unsolicited ARP Request called a “gratuitous ARP”
When a host sends a gratuitous ARP, other hosts on the subnet store the MAC address and IPv4 address contained in the gratuitous ARP in their ARP tables
IP address spoofing is when an attacker hijacks a valid IP address of another device or uses a random IP address

15
ARP Spoofing Attack
The attacker sends two spoofed gratuitous ARP Replies to replace R1 as the default gateway:
 MAC-address CC:CC:CC maps to R1’s

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 10 - LAN Security Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 3 Attack Examples\slide16_shape5.png
images.slide_text:
A host is also allowed to send an unsolicited ARP Request called a “gratuitous ARP”
When a host sends a gratuitous ARP, other hosts on the subnet store the MAC address and IPv4 address contained in the gratuitous ARP in their ARP tables
IP address spoofing is when an attacker hijacks a valid IP address of another device or uses a random IP address

16
ARP Spoofing Attack – Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)
R1 and PC1 remove the correct entry for each other’s MAC address and replace it with PC2’s MAC addr

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 10 - LAN Security Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 3 Attack Examples\slide17_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
MAC address spoofing attacks occur when an attacker alters its MAC address to match another known MAC address of a target host
The attacking host then sends a frame throughout the network with the newly-configured MAC address
When the switch receives the frame, it examines the source MAC address and overwrites the current MAC table entry and assigns the MAC address to the new port
17
Spoofing Attacks

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 10 - LAN Security Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 3 Attack Examples\slide18_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
Attackers can make their hosts appear as root bridges of the spanning tree and therefore capture all traffic for the immediate switched domain
The attacker broadcasts bridge protocol data units (BPDUs) containing configuration and topology changes that will force spanning-tree recalculations
The BPDUs announce a lower bridge priority to be elected as the root bridge
18
Spanning Tree Protocol Attacks

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 10 - LAN Security Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 3 Attack Examples\slide19_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
Cisco Discovery Protocol (CDP) is a proprietary Layer 2 link discovery protocol
CDP can automatically discover other CDP-enabled devices
CDP information is sent out CDP-enabled ports in a periodic, unencrypted multicast
Information includes IP address, IOS version, platform, capabilities, native VLAN
Although useful for network troubleshooting the information provided by CDP can also
  be used to discover vulnerabilities
19
CDP Reconnaissance
A sample Wireshark capture displaying the contents of

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 10 - LAN Security Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 3 Attack Examples\slide20_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
To mitigate the exploitation of CDP, limit the use of CDP on devices or ports
For example, disable CDP on edge ports that connect to untrusted devices
20
CDP Reconnaissance
A sample Wireshark capture displaying the contents of a CDP packet. The attacker can identify the IOS software version and other info.

images.slide_text:
Layer 2 attacks are some of the easiest for hackers 
Threats can be mitigated with some common solutions
All switch ports (interfaces) should be secured before the switch is deployed; e.g., unused ports on a switch



3
Disabling Unused Ports

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 11 - Mitigating Cyber-attacks with Switch Security\_imgs_Lecture 1 Port Security\slide4_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
Some switches implement port security to prevent MAC-address table overflow attacks
Port security allows an administrator to manually configure MAC addresses for a port or to permit the switch to dynamically learn a limited number of MAC addresses
With port security the source MAC address of the frame is compared to the list of secure source MAC addresses manually configured or dynamically learned on the port
By limiting the number of permitted MAC addresses on a port to one, port security can b

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 11 - Mitigating Cyber-attacks with Switch Security\_imgs_Lecture 1 Port Security\slide5_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
Port security is enabled with the switchport port-security interface command
Port security can only be configured on manually configured access ports or manually configured trunk ports
By default, Layer 2 switch ports are set to dynamic auto (trunking on) 
In the example, the port is configured with the switchport mode access interface configuration command

5
Enable Port Security

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 11 - Mitigating Cyber-attacks with Switch Security\_imgs_Lecture 1 Port Security\slide6_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
Use the show port-security interface command to display the settings
If a device is connected to the port, the switch will automatically add the device’s MAC address as a secure MAC. In this example, no device is connected to the port


6
Enable Port Security

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 11 - Mitigating Cyber-attacks with Switch Security\_imgs_Lecture 1 Port Security\slide6_shape5.png
images.slide_text:
Use the show port-security interface command to display the settings

Enable Port Security

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 11 - Mitigating Cyber-attacks with Switch Security\_imgs_Lecture 1 Port Security\slide7_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
The network engineer may want to limit the maximum number of MAC addresses allowed on a port
The default port security value is 1
The maximum number of secure MAC addresses that can be configured depends the switch and operating systems. In this example, the maximum is 8192

7
Limit and Learn MAC Addresses

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 11 - Mitigating Cyber-attacks with Switch Security\_imgs_Lecture 1 Port Security\slide8_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
The switch can be configured to learn MAC addresses in one of three ways:
Manually Configured: the administrator manually configures a static MAC address(es)
Dynamically Learned: the switch learns the MAC address(es) dynamically
Dynamically Learned – Sticky: the switch learns the MAC address(es) dynamically and stores it/them to the running config
Example:
2 MAC addresses maximum
1 MAC address is statically configured
MAC address to be stored on running config
8
Limit and Learn MAC Addresses

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 11 - Mitigating Cyber-attacks with Switch Security\_imgs_Lecture 1 Port Security\slide9_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
Port security aging can be used to set the aging time for static and dynamic addresses
Absolute - The secure addresses are deleted after the specified aging time
Inactivity - The secure addresses are deleted only if they are inactive for the specified aging time
9

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 12 - Routing Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 1 - Introduction to Routing and LPM\slide9_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
The networks in the topology are labeled and highlighted from R1’s perspective
Directly connected networks are connected to the router
Remote networks are not directly connected to the router
A default route  is used when there is no entry in the routing table matching the destination IP


9
Build the Routing Table

pptx_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 12 - Routing Concepts/Lecture 2 - Packet Forwarding.pptx
fixed_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 12 - Routing Concepts/Lecture 2 - Packet Forwarding_fixed.pptx

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 12 - Routing Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 2 - Packet Forwarding\slide3_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
When a packet arrives, the router:
Receives the layer-2 (L2) frame and de-encapsulate the IP packet
Examines the destination IP address
Finds the longest prefix match in the routing table
Encapsulates packet in a L2-frame and forwards it out the egress interface
Drops the packet if the destination IP address does not match any entry


3
Packet Forwarding Decision Process

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 12 - Routing Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 2 - Packet Forwarding\slide4_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
Routers encapsulate packets in the appropriate data link frame type for the outgoing interface


4
End-to-end Packet Forwarding

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 12 - Routing Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 2 - Packet Forwarding\slide5_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
Routers encapsulate packets in the appropriate data link frame type for the outgoing interface


5
End-to-end Packet Forwarding

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 12 - Routing Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 2 - Packet Forwarding\slide6_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
Routers encapsulate packets in the appropriate data link frame type for the outgoing interface


6
End-to-end Packet Forwarding

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 12 - Routing Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 2 - Packet Forwarding\slide8_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
Older (slow) packet forwarding mechanism
A packet is (internally) forwarded to the control plane
CPU matches the destination address with an entry in routing table
It then determines the exit interface and forwards the packet
Forwarding decision is made in software; EVERY packet follows this process, even if the destination is the same for a packet stream


8
Process Switching

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 12 - Routing Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 2 - Packet Forwarding\slide9_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
Fast Switching uses a hardware fast-switching cache memory to store next-hop information
When a packet arrives, it is forwarded to the control plane
The CPU searches for a match in the fast-switching cache; if it is not there, it is process-switched and forwarded to the egress interface 


9
Fast Switching

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 12 - Routing Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 2 - Packet Forwarding\slide10_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
CEF builds a Forwarding Information Base (FIB) and an adjacency table
Table entries are not packet-triggered like fast switching but change-triggered (change in network topology)
When a network has converged, the FIB and adjacency tables contain all information a router needs to forward a packet 


10

Routing Table Entries
A routing table is a data file in RAM
An entry contains the destination network, egress interface, and additional information

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 12 - Routing Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 4 - Understanding the Routing Table\slide8_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
8
Routing Table Entries – Directly Connected Routes
A directly connected network is denoted by a status code of C
The routing table also contains a local route (L) for each of its directly connected networks. This is the IP address that is assigned to the interface

images.slide_text:
7
Structure of an IPv4 Routing Table
In the show ip route output, some route entries are left justified while others are indented
This is because of classful addressing

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 12 - Routing Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 5 - Routing Table Structure and AD\slide8_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
8
Structure of an IPv4 Routing Table
An indented entry is known as a child route. A route entry is indented if it is the subnet of a classful address (class A, B or C)
The child route will include the route source and all the forwarding information such as the next-hop address.


Children routes of the classful route 10.0.0.0/8

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 12 - Routing Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 5 - Routing Table Structure and AD\slide9_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
9
Structure of an IPv4 Routing Table
The classful network address of this subnet will be shown above the route entry, less indented, and without a source code
That route is known as a parent route

Parent route

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 12 - Routing Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 5 - Routing Table Structure and AD\slide10_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
10
Structure of an IPv6 Routing Table
The concept of classful addressing was never part of IPv6, so the structure of an IPv6 routing table is very straight forward
Every IPv6 route entry is formatted and aligned the same way

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 12 - Routing Concepts\_imgs_Lecture 5 - Routing Table Structure and AD\slide12_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
12
Administrative Distance
Routers use what is known as the administrative distance (AD) to determine the route to install into the IP routing table
The lower the AD, the more trustworthy the route source

images.slide_text:
4
Automatically Installed Host Routes
The operating system automatically installs a local host route (L) when an interface address is configured on the router
A host route allows for a more efficient process for packets that are directed to the router itself, rather than for packet forwarding

fixed_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 14 - Troubleshooting Static Routes/Lecture 2 Packet Processing_fixed.pptx

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report/course_content/ITEC445-001-FALL-2025/Module 14 - Troubleshooting Static Routes\_imgs_Lecture 2 Packet Processing\slide3_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
What processes occur when an ICMP packet is generated at PC0, with destination PC1?
ICMP
Address Resolution Protocol
ARP table at PC0 (arp, arp –a, arp –d)
ARP table at router R10 (show arp, clear arp-cache)
IP packet carrying ICMP packet is routed from LAN 10 to LAN 20
De-encapsulation and encapsulation (re-encapsulation) processes at routers
Longest prefix matching at routers


3
Static Routes and Packet Forwarding
